items.correct: Ellipses (Kepler's first law), with the Sun at one focus.
items.why: Students think orbits are circular. Most are nearly circular, but Kepler showed they're ELLIPTICAL - this small difference explains why Earth-Sun distance varies through the year.

items.q: What is the difference between a solar eclipse and a lunar eclipse?
items.correct: In a solar eclipse the Moon blocks the Sun; in a lunar eclipse Earth's shadow covers the Moon.
items.misconception: Solar eclipses happen by day, lunar at night - that is the only difference.
items.why: Students give a superficial answer. The mechanism differs: solar = Moon between Sun and Earth (we're in Moon's shadow); lunar = Earth between Sun and Moon (Moon enters Earth's shadow).

items.q: Why do astronomers infer the existence of dark matter?
items.correct: Visible matter alone cannot account for galaxy rotation curves and gravitational lensing.
items.misconception: Dark matter has been directly observed in telescopes.
items.random_wrong: Because the universe looks dark at night.
items.why: Students think dark matter has been seen. It's INFERRED from gravitational effects on visible matter and light - galaxies rotate too fast for visible mass alone, implying unseen matter contributes most gravity.

items.q: How long is one Earth orbit?
items.why: It's 365.25 days. “1 day” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why do we need leap years?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Real year is 365.25 days. “Tradition” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many days in a leap year?
items.why: Exact answer: 366. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
